title: Gate-dependent offset charge shifts and anharmonicity in gatemon qubits in the weak tunneling regime
authors: Utkan Güngördü, Rusko Ruskov, Silas Hoffman, Kyle Serniak, Andrew J. Kerman, Charles Tahan
abstract: Gatemon qubits are based on a superconductor-quantum dot-superconductor (S-QD-S) junction which enables in situ electrostatic tuning via a gate electrode. For a single-channel QD this structure gives rise to two subgap Andreev bound states (ABSs), and generally leads to a richer quantum phase dynamics as compared to conventional transmons. In a recent work [Phys. Rev. B 111, 214503 (2025)] we derived the quantum phase dynamics from a many-body treatment which leads to an effective gate voltage-dependent Hamiltonian that self-consistently incorporates the phase quantization. It predicts (i) a renormalization of the junction's effective capacitance and (ii) the presence of gate voltage and occupation-dependent charge offsets in junctions with tunneling asymmetry. Here, we quantify the observable impact of these effects on the qubit's energy spectrum and anharmonicity, by studying the interplay of the two Andreev branches as a function of dot-gate voltages and junction transparencies. We show the relation of these predictions to simplified gatemon models and propose a protocol to experimentally detect the predicted charge offsets.
summary: This paper investigates the impact of gate-dependent effects on the performance of gatemon qubits. By using a many-body treatment of S-QD-S junctions, the authors show that tunneling asymmetry leads to new, detectable charge offsets. They also demonstrate that capacitance renormalization plays a crucial role in determining qubit anharmonicity. These findings provide a theoretical framework for understanding and controlling the energy spectrum of gatemon-based quantum processors.
main_problem: Quantifying how gate-dependent offset charge shifts and capacitance renormalization impact the energy spectrum and anharmonicity of gatemon qubits in the weak tunneling regime.
main_result: The study predicts that junction asymmetry induces new gate-voltage-dependent charge offsets and that capacitance renormalization significantly influences qubit anharmonicity, providing a protocol for experimental detection.
method: The authors use a many-body derived effective Hamiltonian and a WKB approach (including an extended WKB method) to solve the Schrödinger equation and analyze Andreev bound state dynamics.
model_or_system: A gatemon qubit consisting of a superconductor-quantum dot-superconductor (S-QD-S) junction in the weak tunneling regime, modeled via an effective Hamiltonian incorporating Andreev bound states and continuum energy shifts.
key_observables: Qubit transition energy oscillations (E01), charge dispersion, qubit anharmonicity, and effective charge offset shifts.
important_parameters: Gate voltage (epsilon_g), tunneling rates (Gamma), junction asymmetry (delta_Gamma), charging energy (EC), and superconducting gap (Delta).
main_assumptions: Weak tunneling regime, slow phase approximation, and the assumption that the even and odd parity sectors are well-separated.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows junction transparency vs. gate voltage; Figure 3 compares WKB theory to exact numerics for charge dispersion; Figure 5 compares relative contributions to anharmonicity; Figure 6 shows numerical anharmonicity vs. gate voltage.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem of gatemon dynamics, presents a many-body derived effective Hamiltonian, applies WKB and numerical methods to study charge dispersion and offsets, analyzes anharmonicity drivers, and concludes with experimental implications.
why_interesting: This work is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it explores how the coupling to a continuum and quantum phase fluctuations lead to renormalization of fundamental qubit parameters like capacitance and charge offsets.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 7. Exact numeric anharmonicity α(ϵg)/EC for ng = 0.6, |ϵg| ≲EC, and ten times smaller EC as compared to Fig. 6(c), such that ∆/EC = 3600, Γ/EC = 160, and δΓ = EC, corresponding to T ≈1 and a true transmon regime (solid blue line). In this case the WKB result of Eq. (40) is ex- ponentially suppressed (horizontal green line), Eq. (A15), and the result is independent of ng. An extended WKB approach (dashed orange line), Appendix A, practically coincides with the exact numerics, in which α(ϵg) < 0 and it never crosses zero line (scf. Eq. (10) of Ref. [8]).
